raw_response: {"rating": -1.2, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report covering a policy speech by the UK Education Secretary on Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND) reform. The author's own prose leans mildly left through sympathetic framing toward SEND families and a subtle attribution of systemic blame to the Conservative-era reforms. The piece rates as Lean Left due to consistent but modest directional choices in the author's connecting language. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing characterizes the current system as one where 'parents have to fight really hard' and where 'councils have been forced to run budget deficits' — language that sympathetically foregrounds the burden on families and institutions rather than maintaining neutral descriptive distance; (2) The embedded article link headline '£100m spent in England on failed efforts to block children's Send support' is an editorial choice that frames Conservative-era policy as adversarial toward disabled children, reinforcing a narrative of institutional failure under the previous government; (3) The author quotes the ASCL president's warning that the system is 'on the brink of collapse' and foregrounds it prominently without a counterbalancing voice defending the current system's achievements; (4) The phrase 'the system that we've inherited from the Conservatives' is attributed to Phillipson, but the author's choice to prominently reproduce this framing without challenge or context serves to anchor blame on the prior government; (5) Source selection skews toward critics demanding more funding (NEU general secretary) with no voice from fiscal conservatives or those skeptical of SEND spending levels, creating a structurally one-sided account."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report covering a policy speech by the UK Education Secretary on Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND) reform. The author's own prose leans mildly left through sympathetic framing toward SEND families and a subtle attribution of systemic blame to the Conservative-era reforms. The piece rates as Lean Left due to consistent but modest directional choices in the author's connecting language. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing characterizes the current system as one where 'parents have to fight really hard' and where 'councils have been forced to run budget deficits' — language that sympathetically foregrounds the burden on families and institutions rather than maintaining neutral descriptive distance; (2) The embedded article link headline '£100m spent in England on failed efforts to block children's Send support' is an editorial choice that frames Conservative-era policy as adversarial toward disabled children, reinforcing a narrative of institutional failure under the previous government; (3) The author quotes the ASCL president's warning that the system is 'on the brink of collapse' and foregrounds it prominently without a counterbalancing voice defending the current system's achievements; (4) The phrase 'the system that we've inherited from the Conservatives' is attributed to Phillipson, but the author's choice to prominently reproduce this framing without challenge or context serves to anchor blame on the prior government; (5) Source selection skews toward critics demanding more funding (NEU general secretary) with no voice from fiscal conservatives or those skeptical of SEND spending levels, creating a structurally one-sided account.

explanation: This is a news report covering a policy speech by the UK Education Secretary on Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND) reform. The author's own prose leans mildly left through sympathetic framing toward SEND families and a subtle attribution of systemic blame to the Conservative-era reforms. The piece rates as Lean Left due to consistent but modest directional choices in the author's connecting language. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing characterizes the current system as one where 'parents have to fight really hard' and where 'councils have been forced to run budget deficits' — language that sympathetically foregrounds the burden on families and institutions rather than maintaining neutral descriptive distance; (2) The embedded article link headline '£100m spent in England on failed efforts to block children's Send support' is an editorial choice that frames Conservative-era policy as adversarial toward disabled children, reinforcing a narrative of institutional failure under the previous government; (3) The author quotes the ASCL president's warning that the system is 'on the brink of collapse' and foregrounds it prominently without a counterbalancing voice defending the current system's achievements; (4) The phrase 'the system that we've inherited from the Conservatives' is attributed to Phillipson, but the author's choice to prominently reproduce this framing without challenge or context serves to anchor blame on the prior government; (5) Source selection skews toward critics demanding more funding (NEU general secretary) with no voice from fiscal conservatives or those skeptical of SEND spending levels, creating a structurally one-sided account.
